title: COVID-19 origins: was the Huanan market the early epicentre?
question: Do the early-case residential locations show the Huanan market was the epicentre, or is that clustering an artefact of ascertainment bias?

sources.title: Confirmation of the centrality of the Huanan market among early COVID-19 cases

reliability.knownStance: argues the Huanan market is the early epicentre

sources.title: Proximity Ascertainment Bias in Early Covid Case Locations

reliability.knownStance: argues proximity ascertainment bias makes the market clustering unreliable

sources.title: No evidence of systematic proximity ascertainment bias in early COVID-19 cases, Reply to Weissman

passages.verbatimText: The centrality of Wuhan's Huanan market in maps of December 2019 COVID-19 case residential locations, established by Worobey et al. (2022a), has recently been challenged by Stoyan and Chiu (2024, SC2024).

SC2024 proposed a statistical test based on the premise that the measure of central tendency (hereafter, "centre") of a sample of case locations must coincide with the exact point from which local transmission began.

SC2024 put forward two alternative centres (centroid and mode) to the centre-point which was used by Worobey et al. for some analyses, and proposed a bootstrapping method, based on their premise, to test whether a particular location is consistent with it being the point source of transmission.
We show that SC2024's concerns about the use of centre-points are inconsequential, and that use of centroids for these data is inadvisable.
The mode is an appropriate, even optimal, choice as centre;

however, contrary to SC2024's results, we demonstrate that with proper implementation of their methods, the mode falls at the entrance of a parking lot at the market itself, and the 95% confidence region around the mode includes the market.
Thus, the market cannot be rejected as central even by SC2024's overly stringent statistical test.

Our results directly contradict SC2024's and -- together with myriad additional lines of evidence overlooked by SC2024, including crucial epidemiological information -- point to the Huanan market as the early epicentre of the COVID-19 pandemic.

A comparison of the distances to the Huanan Seafood Market of early Covid cases with known links to the market versus cases without known links shows results apparently incompatible with a location model lacking proximity ascertainment bias.
The sign of the difference instead agrees with a model in which such ascertainment bias is large.
In the presence of such bias inferences based on the clustering of case locations become unreliable.

Weissman (2024) argues that the finding that early COVID-19 cases without an ascertained link to Wuhan's Huanan Seafood Wholesale market resided on average closer to the market than cases epidemiologically linked to it, reveals "major proximity ascertainment bias".
Here we show that Weissman's conclusion is based on a flawed premise, and that there is no such "internal evidence" of major bias.
The pattern can indeed be explained by places of infection not being limited to residential neighbourhoods, and by stochasticity -- i.e., without requiring any ascertainment bias.

claims.statement: The centrality of Wuhan's Huanan market in maps of December 2019 COVID-19 case residential locations, established by Worobey et al. (2022a), has recently been challenged by Stoyan and Chiu (2024, SC2024).

SC2024 proposed a statistical test based on the premise that the measure of central tendency (hereafter, "centre") of a sample of case locations must coincide with the exact point from which local transmission began.

SC2024 put forward two alternative centres (centroid and mode) to the centre-point which was used by Worobey et al. for some analyses, and proposed a bootstrapping method, based on their premise, to test whether a particular location is consistent with it being the point source of transmission.
We show that SC2024's concerns about the use of centre-points are inconsequential, and that use of centroids for these data is inadvisable.
The mode is an appropriate, even optimal, choice as centre;

Contrary to SC2024's results, we demonstrate that with proper implementation of their methods, the mode falls at the entrance of a parking lot at the market itself, and the 95% confidence region around the mode includes the market.
Thus, the market cannot be rejected as central even by SC2024's overly stringent statistical test.

Our results directly contradict SC2024's and -- together with myriad additional lines of evidence overlooked by SC2024, including crucial epidemiological information -- point to the Huanan market as the early epicentre of the COVID-19 pandemic.

A comparison of the distances to the Huanan Seafood Market of early Covid cases with known links to the market versus cases without known links shows results apparently incompatible with a location model lacking proximity ascertainment bias.
The sign of the difference instead agrees with a model in which such ascertainment bias is large.
In the presence of such bias inferences based on the clustering of case locations become unreliable.

Weissman (2024) argues that the finding that early COVID-19 cases without an ascertained link to Wuhan's Huanan Seafood Wholesale market resided on average closer to the market than cases epidemiologically linked to it, reveals "major proximity ascertainment bias".
Weissman's conclusion is based on a flawed premise, and there is no such "internal evidence" of major bias.
The pattern can be explained by places of infection not being limited to residential neighbourhoods and by stochasticity, without requiring any ascertainment bias.
The Huanan market was the early epicentre of the COVID‑19 pandemic.

inferences.warrant: If the mode location and its 95 % confidence interval include the market, then the market cannot be statistically rejected as the central point of early transmission.
Demonstrating that the market cannot be rejected as central provides direct support for it being the early epicentre of the pandemic.
defeaters: Centrality does not necessarily imply origin

inferences.warrant: Finding that the empirically derived mode falls at the market entrance validates the mode as an appropriate (and optimal) centre measure for these data.
defeaters: Alternative centre measures perform equally well

inferences.warrant: If concerns about centre‑points are inconsequential and centroids are inadvisable, then SC2024’s proposed alternative centres (centroid and mode) lack relevance for the analysis.
defeaters: SC2024’s alternative centres are theoretically justified

inferences.warrant: If proximity ascertainment bias makes clustering inferences unreliable, then SC2024’s bootstrapping test that assumes unbiased clustering is undermined.
defeaters: Bias is negligible in the dataset
The test is robust to bias

inferences.warrant: The observed distance patterns are incompatible with a model lacking bias and align with a model that incorporates large proximity ascertainment bias, indicating that such bias is present.
defeaters: Alternative stochastic explanations fit the data

inferences.warrant: Weissman’s claim of major proximity ascertainment bias rests on a flawed premise; therefore his conclusion about bias is invalid.
The pattern can be explained without invoking ascertainment bias, reinforcing that Weissman’s premise (and thus his bias claim) is flawed.
defeaters: The non‑bias explanation fails to account for all observations

inferences.warrant: Earlier mapping that showed the market as the central location of early cases provides empirical support for the market being the epicentre.
defeaters: The mapping suffers from its own ascertainment bias

inferences.warrant: Our results directly point to the Huanan market as the early epicentre, adding strong empirical support to that conclusion.
If proximity ascertainment bias makes residential-clustering inferences unreliable, the clustering cannot establish the market as the early epicentre.
defeaters: The bias is shown to be small or absent
Non-spatial lines of evidence independently establish the epicentre

challenges.rationale: SC2024’s statistical test rests on the strong premise that the geographic centre of a sample of case residences must coincide exactly with the point of origin of local transmission, yet the authors provide no qualification for stochastic spread, multiple introductions, or reporting delays that can decouple the two, making the premise overly simplistic.
challenges.suggestedRemedy: Add a discussion of the conditions under which the centre‑of‑cases could approximate the source, and acknowledge situations where it would not.

challenges.rationale: The claim treats the centroid and mode, measures of central tendency, as if they were candidate point‑source locations, extending their statistical meaning beyond what the data support; a centre does not necessarily identify the origin of transmission.
challenges.suggestedRemedy: Clarify that centroid/mode are descriptive summaries, not causal source estimates, or provide additional analysis linking these summaries to source inference.

challenges.rationale: The inference that ‘the market cannot be rejected as central’ directly supports the market being the early epicentre conflates statistical non‑rejection with causal attribution; a location’s centrality does not prove it was the origin of the outbreak.
challenges.suggestedRemedy: Separate the statistical result from the causal claim and supply independent epidemiological evidence to substantiate the epicentre hypothesis.

challenges.rationale: The statement that ‘Weissman's conclusion is based on a flawed premise’ is made without citing any source that demonstrates the flaw, leaving the claim unsupported.
challenges.suggestedRemedy: Provide a specific reference or analysis that details the alleged flaw in Weissman's premise.

challenges.rationale: Concluding that the Huanan market was the early epicentre relies primarily on spatial clustering evidence, which does not by itself establish causality or exclude alternative origins such as multiple simultaneous introductions.
challenges.suggestedRemedy: Incorporate virological, phylogenetic, and broader epidemiological data, or qualify the conclusion as a hypothesis pending further evidence.

challenges.rationale: The inference that earlier mapping showing the market as the central location of early cases provides empirical support for it being the epicentre stretches the evidence; centrality in a map does not equate to being the source of the outbreak.
challenges.suggestedRemedy: Limit the inference to describing spatial patterns and avoid using it as direct proof of the market’s role as the epicentre, or supplement with additional causal evidence.

correlationGroups.rationale: Claims from 2 sources by overlapping authors (florence débarre, michael worobey) not independent evidence: "Confirmation of the centrality of the H…"; "No evidence of systematic proximity asc…".

matches.rationale: Claim 1 states SC2024's premise for the test, while Claim 2 describes the specific alternative centres and bootstrapping method they proposed; they address different aspects of the same study.
Claim 4 asserts that the mode is the optimal centre, a stronger, more specific claim than Claim 2's broader suggestion that the mode is merely an alternative centre.
Claim 3 says using centroids is inadvisable, directly opposing Claim 2 which presents centroids as a viable alternative proposed by SC2024.
Claim 5 reports results that contradict SC2024's findings reported in Claim 2 about the location of the mode.
Claim 4 declares the mode optimal, while Claim 5 provides empirical evidence about the mode’s location; the claims are consistent but address different dimensions (normative vs empirical).
Claim 1 outlines the test premise, whereas Claim 6 reports the outcome of applying that test; they are compatible but focus on premise versus result.
Claim 5 gives a specific location finding that supports the broader assertion in Claim 7 that the market was the early epicentre.
Claim 6 states the market cannot be rejected as central, a specific test result that underlies the broader epicentre claim of Claim 7.
Claim 9 specifies a model with a large ascertainment bias, which is a more detailed version of Claim 8’s broader statement that results are incompatible with a bias‑free model.
Claim 10 makes a general claim about the unreliability of clustering inferences under bias, encompassing the specific distance‑based result described in Claim 8.
Claim 11 argues for a major proximity ascertainment bias, while Claim 12 denies any such bias; the two statements cannot both be true.
Claim 11 posits a substantial bias, whereas Claim 13 explains the pattern without invoking any bias, directly opposing each other.
Both claims reject the presence of bias, but Claim 12 attacks the premise of bias, while Claim 13 offers an alternative explanation; they are compatible but address different aspects.
Claim 8 indicates results are incompatible with a no‑bias model, implying bias exists, which aligns with Claim 11’s assertion of major bias; they are consistent but focus on different evidence.
Both Claim 9 and Claim 11 describe the presence of a large ascertainment bias; they are compatible though one discusses model sign and the other describes empirical bias.
Claim 10 discusses the effect of bias on inference reliability, while Claim 11 asserts the existence of such bias; the statements are compatible but address cause versus consequence.
Claim 12 denies any major bias, whereas Claim 8’s incompatibility with a bias‑free model implies bias is present; the claims contradict each other.
Claim 13 explains the pattern without bias, directly opposing Claim 8’s implication that bias must be present.
Claim 13 rejects the need for bias, while Claim 9 asserts a large bias model fits the data; these are mutually exclusive.
Claim 13 denies bias, whereas Claim 10 states that bias makes clustering inferences unreliable; they cannot both be true.
Claim 0 says the market’s centrality has been challenged, while Claim 7 asserts the market is the early epicentre, directly opposing the challenge.
Claim 0 reports a challenge to the market’s centrality, whereas Claim 6 concludes the market cannot be rejected as central; the two statements contradict each other.

overlays.label: Market-central reading
overlays.description: Reads the residential-clustering evidence as robust: the mode is a sound centre that falls at the market, and the ascertainment-bias objection is not established, so it does not undercut the epicentre conclusion.
overlays.priorStance: The early-case clustering points to the Huanan market as the epicentre.

overlays.label: Ascertainment-bias reading
overlays.description: Holds that proximity ascertainment bias is real and large, so residential-clustering inferences are unreliable and cannot carry the epicentre conclusion; doubts the stochasticity rescue and reads a mode at the market as partly an artefact of where cases were sought.
overlays.priorStance: The clustering may be an artefact of where cases were sought; centrality is not established.

assessments.rationale: With proper implementation the mode falls at the market entrance and the 95% region includes it.
The Worobey et al. centrality result is sound.
Infection away from home plus chance explains the pattern without any bias.
The distance comparison is not compelling once infection locations are not assumed residential.
The sign is consistent with ordinary spread, not with large bias.
The bias is not established, so it does not undercut centrality.
The closer-than-linked pattern is the signature of proximity ascertainment bias.
The sign of the distance difference matches a large-bias model.
Stochasticity is asserted, not shown sufficient to explain the pattern.
A mode at the market may itself reflect where cases were ascertained.
Centrality is exactly what is contested.
If the clustering is unreliable, it cannot establish the market as epicentre.

quarantine.statement: We show that this premise is erroneous.
quarantine.attemptedPassageText: We show that this premise is erroneous.

sourceDocument.text:
Confirmation of the centrality of the Huanan market among early COVID-19 cases (Débarre and Worobey, 2024).

The centrality of Wuhan's Huanan market in maps of December 2019 COVID-19 case residential locations, established by Worobey et al. (2022a), has recently been challenged by Stoyan and Chiu (2024, SC2024). SC2024 proposed a statistical test based on the premise that the measure of central tendency (hereafter, "centre") of a sample of case locations must coincide with the exact point from which local transmission began. Here we show that this premise is erroneous. SC2024 put forward two alternative centres (centroid and mode) to the centre-point which was used by Worobey et al. for some analyses, and proposed a bootstrapping method, based on their premise, to test whether a particular location is consistent with it being the point source of transmission. We show that SC2024's concerns about the use of centre-points are inconsequential, and that use of centroids for these data is inadvisable. The mode is an appropriate, even optimal, choice as centre; however, contrary to SC2024's results, we demonstrate that with proper implementation of their methods, the mode falls at the entrance of a parking lot at the market itself, and the 95% confidence region around the mode includes the market. Thus, the market cannot be rejected as central even by SC2024's overly stringent statistical test. Our results directly contradict SC2024's and -- together with myriad additional lines of evidence overlooked by SC2024, including crucial epidemiological information -- point to the Huanan market as the early epicentre of the COVID-19 pandemic.
sourceDocument.title: Confirmation of the centrality of the Huanan market among early COVID-19 cases
